source_texts: The tiny figure stopped, and imploringly stretched out toward the phosphorescent intervals between the branches, which were forming the body.

“I mean those clubs, those Dussot’s, and those spits of land,§ perhaps, that’s progress for you—well, that’ll all be without me,” he continued, once more taking no note of the question. “Besides, who wants to be a cardsharp?”

The famous doctor said his goodbyes with a serious expression, but one that hadn’t given up hope. And to the timid question Ivan Ilyich put to him, raising eyes that were shining with fear and hope—is there any possibility of recovery?—he answered that though one couldn’t guarantee it, there was a possibility. The look of hope with which Ivan Ilyich said goodbye to the doctor was so pitiful that, when she saw it, Praskovya Fyodorovna burst into tears as she went through the study doors to give the famous doctor his fee.

The princesses climbed onto their royal elephants, and since Kundavai had already hinted that those leaving for a long journey must start on their journey first, Vandiya Devan turned the horse’s head towards the road albeit with great hesitation. Even then he couldn’t help but turn towards her and look upon her beautiful face once again, but realizing that it is unmannerly, he turned the horse’s head swiftly and galloped away within a flash. The Vaidiyar’s son, who was to accompany him, struggled to control his horse and followed him.

He followed the road-mender’s advice, went back, and half an hour later drove past the same spot but this time at a fast trot with a good trace horse. The horse-handler, who called himself a postilion, sat on the cart shaft.
“Thandavaraya, I don’t think this is a real horse. It may belong to the poi kaal kudirai (false horse performance) group,” said one of the soldiers. “Fine then.

the young woman thought with a proud flutter. She had waited for this moment to illuminate her entire life and wanted to rise high up where she could gaze upon that dark path she had walked along yesterday, all alone and inconspicuous.

She explained to him what had happened, that Fantine had been very poorly the day before, and that she was better now because she thought that monsieur le maire had gone to Montfermeil to fetch her child. The nun dared not question monsieur le maire, but from the way he looked she could see quite clearly that was not where he had come from.
“For me the Prince happens to be the first person in my life in whom I recognized a truly loyal person. He believed in me from the first, and I believe in him.”

The truth is that no one has ever really explained how Joseph Buquet met his death. The verdict at the inquest was ‘suicide under natural circumstances’. In his Memoirs of a Theatre Director, Armand Moncharmin, one of the co-directors who succeeded Messrs Debienne and Poligny, relates the incident as follows: A most unfortunate accident ruined the little party that Messrs Debienne and Poligny were giving to mark their departure.
rationales: Outputs 3 and 4 both use the distinctive phrase 'phosphorescent intervals' instead of 'glowing spaces/gaps' used by the others. They also both use formal/archaic synonyms for 'pleadingly' ('imploringly' and 'in supplication'), and both end with a similar minimal clause 'that formed the body/a body' rather than expanding on the formation metaphor. These shared stylistic and lexical fingerprints strongly suggest they come from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 3 and 4 share the same unusual structural inversion (placing 'who wants to be a cardsharp?' before the main quote), the same 'Dussots' spelling, 'spits of land' phrasing, 'I mean those/these clubs' construction, and 'once more' + [ignoring/not noticing] the question. Their differences are minor word-level variations consistent with the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 0 and 1 share nearly identical structure, narrative sequence, and vocabulary. Both begin with 'The look of hope with which Ivan Ilyich said farewell/goodbye to the doctor was so pitiful,' use 'famous doctor,' describe Praskovya Fyodorovna bursting into tears in the same position in the narrative, and follow the same sentence-by-sentence ordering of events. The differences are minor surface-level word choices (farewell vs. goodbye, slight rephrasing of the doctor's response), consistent with two outputs from the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 2 and 4 share nearly identical structure and vocabulary: both open with princesses mounting royal elephants, both use 'subtly suggested/subtle suggestion,' both describe Vandiya Devan stealing 'one final glance at her lovely/beautiful face,' and both conclude with the Vaidiyar's son 'struggling/wrestling' with his horse. These parallel phrasings and the same sequence of narrative beats strongly indicate they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 3 share the same distinctive structural inversion — both begin with a sentence describing the postilion seated on the cart shaft before describing the protagonist following advice. Both also use 'trace horse,' 'half an hour later,' and 'fast trot,' which are unique phrasing choices not found in the other outputs. This combination of restructured sentence order and matching vocabulary strongly indicates they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 4 share the same structural pattern: a direct quote format, preservation of the Tamil term 'poi kaal/kal kudirai' (with only minor spelling variation), and attribution to 'one of the soldiers said.' Both maintain the same core meaning and sentence rhythm, differing only in the address ('Thandavaraya' vs 'Thambi'), which is a small variation typical of the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 4 share the same sentence structure, nearly identical vocabulary choices (illumine/illuminate, gaze on/gaze down, traversed/walked, alone and unseen/alone and inconspicuous), and the same overall flow. Both preserve 'waited for that moment,' the light metaphor, the desire to go high and look down, and the 'dark path' walked 'alone' — with only minor synonym substitutions between them, indicating the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 2 and 3 share the same distinctive structural reordering — both begin with 'The nun did not dare [to] question the mayor' followed by the observation about his demeanor, then transition to explaining Fantine's illness. This contrasts with outputs 0 and 1 which follow the original source order (Fantine's illness first, nun's observation last). The sentence-by-sentence construction in 2 and 3 is nearly identical, with only minor word-choice variations ('dare to' vs 'dare', 'by his look' vs 'by the way he looked', 'take' vs 'fetch'), which is consistent with the same paraphraser producing slightly varied outputs.

Outputs 1 and 3 share nearly identical sentence structure and phrasing: 'the first person in my life whom I recognized as a truly loyal [person/man]' and 'He believed/has believed in me from the very beginning, and I believe in him.' The only differences are minor word choices ('person' vs 'man', 'believed' vs 'has believed') and quotation mark style (single vs double). This level of structural and lexical similarity strongly suggests they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 4 share nearly identical structural and stylistic patterns: both open with 'Nobody has ever [adverb] explained how Joseph Buquet died,' both place the verdict in quotation marks as 'suicide due to natural causes,' both title the book as 'Memoirs of a Theatre Director,' both describe Moncharmin as 'one of the directors who took over from Debienne and Poligny,' and both use 'describes the event' as the transitional phrase before the quoted passage. These consistent choices across multiple linguistic dimensions strongly suggest they came from the same paraphraser.

human_par3: Left with no further pretext to dawdle, Vandhiyathevan turned his horse—with a great deal of reluctance, it must be admitted. He craned his neck and gazed at Kundhavai one last time, eyes fairly brimming with eagerness and yearning. Then, abruptly, he delivered a slap to his horse, as though furious with it; that well-bred animal sprang forward, quite unused to such cavalier treatment. It sped away, kicking up a cloud of dust; the Vaidhyar's son had to gather all his considerable inexperience just to follow him.
llama_31_8b: As the elephant-mounted princesses prepared to depart, Vandiya Devan heeded Kundavai's subtle advice that travelers embarking on lengthy journeys should leave first. Thus, he reluctantly guided his horse towards the road, fighting the urge to glance back. Despite his initial hesitation, he ultimately yielded to the urge to take one final look at the princess, but quickly suppressed the gesture, instead accelerating his horse and galloping away swiftly. His companion, the Vaidiyar's son, struggled to manage his own horse and followed closely behind.

The nun did not dare question the mayor, but by the way he looked she clearly saw that he had not come from there. She explained to him what had happened, that Fantine had been very ill the day before, and that now she was better because she thought that the mayor had gone to Montfermeil to fetch her child.
“The prince is the first person in my life whom I recognized as a truly loyal man. He has believed in me from the very beginning, and I believe in him.”

The circumstances surrounding Joseph Buquet's death remain unclear. An inquest concluded that he had taken his own life under normal circumstances. In his memoirs, Armand Moncharmin recounts the incident: A unfortunate accident marred the farewell party hosted by Debienne and Poligny.
The figure stopped and reached out in supplication to the phosphorescent intervals between the branches, that formed a body:

“Besides, who wants to be a cardsharp?” “I mean those clubs, those Dussots, and those spits of land—if that’s progress for you—well, all that’s without me,” he continued, once more not noticing the question.

The celebrated specialist took his leave looking serious but not hopeless. And in answer to the question put to him diffidently by Ivan Ilyich, whose eyes were glistening with fear and hope, as to whether there was any possibility of recovery, he replied that nothing could be guaranteed but there was a possibility. The look of hope on Ivan Ilyich’s face as he watched the doctor leave was so pathetic that when she saw it Praskovya actually burst into tears as she walked out of the study to give the celebrated specialist his fee.

The princesses mounted their royal elephants. Following Kundavai's subtle suggestion that those embarking on extended travels ought to depart first, Vandiya Devan, though reluctant, guided his horse towards the path. He couldn't resist one final glance at her lovely face, but quickly, deeming it impolite, he sharply turned his horse and galloped off. The Vaidiyar's son, meant to travel with him, wrestled with his own horse and chased after him.

Heeding the road-mender's suggestion, he turned his cart around and returned to the scene, this time passing by at a brisk pace with a strong horse leading the way. On the cart, a self-proclaimed specialist in handling horses was perched on the shafts.
“Thambi, this is no real horse. I think it belongs to the poi kal kudirai troupe,” one of the soldiers said.
She had waited for that moment, which would illuminate her whole life, and she wanted to rise high enough to gaze down on the dark path along which she had walked yesterday, alone and inconspicuous.

Fantine's condition had taken a turn for the worse the previous day, but she had shown a slight improvement, thanks to a misunderstanding. It seemed she believed that the mayor had gone to retrieve her child from Montfermeil, which had lifted her spirits. The nun was hesitant to pry into the mayor's affairs, but his demeanor suggested that he was not telling the truth about his recent travels.
I've found the Prince to be the most steadfastly loyal individual I've ever encountered. From the very beginning, he's shown unwavering faith in me, and I, in turn, have complete trust in him.

Nobody has ever definitively explained how Joseph Buquet died. The official ruling at his inquest was "suicide due to natural causes." In his book, *Memoirs of a Theatre Director*, Armand Moncharmin, one of the directors who took over from Debienne and Poligny, describes the event this way: A terrible incident disrupted the farewell party that Debienne and Poligny were hosting.
